title: Nine Cities Compete to Be UK City of Culture 2029
summary: Nine cities in the UK are trying to become the City of Culture for 2029. They are Blackpool, Inverness and the Highlands, Ipswich, Middlesbrough, Milton Keynes, Portsmouth, Sheffield, Swindon, and Wrexham. The government chose them and gave each city £60,000 to make a plan. The winner will get £10 million to do fun cultural activities for a year. The next three best cities will get £125,000 each. The Culture Secretary, Lisa Nandy, said this helps share opportunities across the country. A writer named Sir Phil Redmond leads the group that picks the winner. He says culture brings people together and makes cities proud. Past winners like Coventry made lots of money and had more visitors. This competition is exciting for everyone!

keywords.explanation: a title given to a UK city to celebrate its arts and culture for one year
the group of people who make rules and decisions for the country
money given to help pay for projects or activities
a government leader in charge of culture and media
a contest where people or places try to win something
fun things to do, like art shows or festivals

What is the prize money for the winning city?

What do the next three best cities receive?
What did Sir Phil Redmond say culture does for cities?
Which city hosted the Turner Prize exhibition last year?

background_read: The UK City of Culture is a special award given every few years to a city in the United Kingdom. It helps that city show off its art, music, and local stories. The government runs this competition to make sure all parts of the country get a chance to shine. Past winners like Coventry and Hull had big festivals that brought in visitors and money. This makes cities happier and more famous. It's like a big party that everyone can join!
Article_Structure: WHO: The UK government, Culture Secretary Lisa Nandy, writer Sir Phil Redmond, and nine cities including Blackpool and Ipswich.
WHAT: A competition to choose the UK City of Culture for 2029, with funding for bids and prizes for winners.
WHERE: Across the UK, in cities like Blackpool, Inverness, and Sheffield.
WHY: To share opportunities, celebrate culture, bring people together, and help cities grow through arts and activities.

perspectives.description: The government sees this as a way to spread opportunities and recognize cultural contributions across the UK.
Cities use this chance to showcase their unique stories and boost local pride and economy.
